mnemonics.title: Compare Return Value
mnemonics.content: Negative: First < Second | Zero: First = Second | Positive: First > Second - Think: N-Z-P (like NZP = Not Zero = Positive)

mnemonics.title: Comparable vs Comparator
mnemonics.content: Comparable = I AM comparable (internal, modify class) | Comparator = I COMPARE for you (external, don't modify)

mnemonics.title: nullsFirst vs nullsLast
mnemonics.content: nullsFirst: NULL is FIRST (at beginning) = MIN value | nullsLast: NULL is LAST (at end) = MAX value

mnemonics.title: thenComparing Chain Order
mnemonics.content: Primary . Secondary . Tertiary - Think: Compare First, THEN compare Second for ties

mnemonics.title: Comparing Methods
mnemonics.content: comparing() → Object | comparingInt() → int | comparingLong() → long | comparingDouble() → double

acronyms.expansion: Single Abstract Method - Comparator has compare() as its SAM
acronyms.usage: Used with lambda expressions for concise comparator creation

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - naturalOrder() for Queue/LinkedList ordering
acronyms.usage: When you want elements processed in insertion order

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a layered cake: comparing() is base layer, thenComparing() adds layers on top. Each layer only matters when the layer below finds equal items
Think of null as the smallest element (-∞). nullsFirst() puts null at position 0. nullsLast() puts null at the end (like +∞)
Think of a mirror (reversed) - ascending becomes descending. The mirror reflects the entire sorting logic

analogies.analogy: Comparable is like a person knowing their own rank in a line. Comparator is like a judge standing outside ranking people by specific criteria without them knowing
It's like sorting a class: first by Roll Number (primary), if same then by Name (secondary), if still same then by Age (tertiary)
Lambda (a,b) -> a-b is like giving a short instruction to a sorter: 'subtract b from a' tells direction and magnitude
